exemplar.title: To What Extent Does Rutherford-Geiger-Marsden Scattering Serve as a Method that Accurately Obtains the Radius of the Nucleus of Gold in Comparison to the Davisson-Germer Method of Electron Diffraction, with Reference to its Literature Value Yielded by the âNational Nuclear Data Centerâ?

detailedExemplarData.title: To What Extent Does Rutherford-Geiger-Marsden Scattering Serve as a Method that Accurately Obtains the Radius of the Nucleus of Gold in Comparison to the Davisson-Germer Method of Electron Diffraction, with Reference to its Literature Value Yielded by the âNational Nuclear Data Centerâ?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated and tightly linked to the subsequent analysis and discussion.
Topic is presented with discipline-specific relevance and anchored by well-chosen literature references.
Methodology is generally complete, with relevant theoretical techniques and parameters described in tables and schematics.
weaknesses: The research question and title are overly verbose and contain multiple clauses, which slightly obscures focus.
Justification for the simulated datasets (calibration, validation and potential biases) is too brief and under-developed.
Minor digressions and repeated definitions dilute the clarity of the introduction.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a clear and effective focus throughout, with a sharply articulated investigation question and coherent methodological planning. Minor issues of verbosity and incomplete methodological justification prevent full marks, placing this criterion firmly in the 5/6 band.

strengths: Technical terminology (e.g. âimpact parameterâ, âdifferential cross-sectionâ, âRayleigh criterionâ) is used accurately and consistently.
Subject knowledge is applied soundly, linking Coulombâs law, de Broglie relations and relativistic corrections to the investigation.
Mathematical notation and symbols follow standard physics conventions and support the theoretical narrative.
weaknesses: A few conceptual oversights (e.g. neglect of electron screening, treatment of kinetic vs rest energy) remain unaddressed.
Certain relations (e.g. cross-section energy dependence) are asserted without full derivation.
Typographical errors and occasional awkward syntax slightly impede the flow of complex explanations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student displays an excellent command of physics concepts and terminology, with only minor inaccuracies and omissions. This consistent, high-level application of subject knowledge warrants a 5/6.

strengths: Analysis of scattering angles and diffraction minima is clearly tied back to the research question.
The comparative argument between Rutherford scattering and electron diffraction is logically structured and supported by data.
Sources of uncertainty (statistical vs systematic) are identified and discussed in the context of result reliability.
weaknesses: Critical evaluation remains largely qualitative; quantitative sensitivity or uncertainty propagation is not performed.
Some conclusions (e.g. relative frailty of electron diffraction) lack broader theoretical or numerical justification.
The discussion occasionally slips into descriptive narrative rather than deeper critical synthesis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report shows good research analysis and a coherent, comparative argument, with evidence of critical evaluation. However, the lack of quantitative depth and full critical rigour limits this to the middle of the 0â12 range, justifying a score of 5/12.

strengths: Overall structure follows Extended Essay conventions (TOC, clear headings, bibliography, word count within limits).
Figures, tables and graphs are numbered and captioned, aiding navigation and reader understanding.
Mathematical notation is typeset clearly, and visual elements generally support the text.
weaknesses: Equation numbering and figure caption styles are inconsistent across the document.
Several images are low resolution and some captions include in-text URLs rather than standard bibliographic references.
Long headings, awkward figure placement and inconsistent citation formatting slightly disrupt the polish of the presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is well organized and meets most presentation conventions, but recurrent formatting inconsistencies and polish issues prevent the top mark. A middle-band score of 3/4 accurately reflects this level of presentation quality.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Strengths:**  Technical vocabularyââimpact parameterâ, âdifferential cross-sectionâ, âLorentz factorâ, âRayleigh criterionâ, etc.âis used confidently and usually accurately.  Mathematical symbolism is appropriate and generally consistent with standard physics notation.

**Limitations:**  Sporadic typographical errors and some over-elaborate phrasing occur, and the long research question illustrates occasional awkward syntax.  Nonetheless, misconceptions are minimal and do not impede comprehension.

**Overall judgment:**  Terminology and conceptual usage are accurate and sustained, placing the work in the 5-6 band; **5/6** is awarded.

**Strengths:**  A comparative argument is structured across sections, culminating in a tabulated evaluation of both methods.  Reasoning generally follows from data, and the student references theoretical principles when justifying which technique is âmore accurateâ.

**Limitations:**  The argumentative line is somewhat narrative and repetitive, occasionally substituting description for critical synthesis.  The conclusion restates results but does not probe alternative interpretations or broader theoretical implications in depth.

**Overall judgment:**  The argument is coherent and generally well developed, yet not sophisticated; this aligns with the middle of the **4-6 band**.  A score of **5/12** is appropriate.

**Strengths:**  The essay outlines, with equations and schematics, how each technique (scattering cross-sections, de Broglie wavelength, Rayleigh criterion) will be used to calculate nuclear radius.  Key constants, assumptions and simulated apparatus are described, and tables list the parameters varied.

**Limitations:**  The methodology remains largely *theoretical*; no primary data were gathered, and the simulationsâ settings, calibration and validation are only briefly justified.  Critical procedural details (e.g., how uncertainties in simulations were generated, how minima were located algorithmically) are not fully specified.  Consequently, completeness and evidence of *effective* methodological choice are uneven.

**Overall judgment:**  The approach is generally sound and mostly complete but lacks depth in practical justification and validation, matching the **3-4 band**; the higher value **4/6** is awarded.

**Strengths:**  The student demonstrates solid understanding of nuclear and quantum physics, correctly employing Coulombâs law, scattering cross-sections, de Broglie relations and relativistic corrections.  Explanations link theory to experimental context, and citations show engagement with authoritative sources.

**Limitations:**  A few conceptual simplifications are left unchallenged (e.g., complete neglect of electron screening in diffraction, assumption that 27.5 MeV Î±-particles do not experience the strong force).  Nevertheless, such oversights do not substantially undermine the generally accurate application of physics principles.

**Overall judgment:**  Knowledge is consistently sound and usually well-applied, meriting the upper band (5-6); a score of **5/6** reflects occasional minor inaccuracies.

**Strengths:**  The student identifies several sources of error (statistical fluctuations, strong-force intrusion, electron screening) and comments on simulation limitations, indicating an emerging critical stance.

**Limitations:**  Evaluation remains largely qualitative; no quantitative assessment (e.g., propagated uncertainty, sensitivity analysis) is attempted.  The critique is surface-level and sometimes accepts model assumptions without challenging their impact on findings.

**Overall judgment:**  Evidence of critical evaluation exists but is uneven and limited, matching the lower end of the **4-6 band**; **4/12** is awarded.

**Strengths:**  The student identifies a precise, discipline-relevant topic from nuclear physics and states it early, both as a full title and as a clearly sign-posted research question.  The introduction explains why comparing Rutherford-Geiger-Marsden scattering with Davisson-Germer electron diffraction is worth investigating, and key terms (e.g., ânuclear radius of goldâ, âliterature valueâ) are defined.

**Limitations:**  While the purpose is clear, the phrasing of the research question is excessively long and occasionally cumbersome, which slightly obscures its focus.  Occasional digressions (historical anecdotes, repeated definitions) dilute the sharpness of communication.

**Overall judgment:**  Clarity, relevance and focus are consistently evident, but minor verbosity prevents a flawless execution; the work best fits the upper band (5-6) and is awarded **5/6**.

**Strengths:**  Numerous figures, tables and graphs are included, each numbered and captioned; the table of contents and headings aid navigation.  Mathematical expressions are typeset clearly, and colours/shapes in graphs help readability.

**Limitations:**  Several images are low-resolution, some captions are repetitive, and figure references occasionally mis-match (e.g., Figure 10/11 confusion).  White-space and alignment vary, and long equations wrap awkwardly in places.

**Overall judgment:**  Visual presentation generally enhances communication, though imperfections remain; this fits comfortably within the upper band (3-4).  A score of **3/4** is awarded.

**Strengths:**  The research question is explicitly stated, narrowly targets the determination of the gold nucleus radius and anchors the ensuing discussion, tables and graphs.  Sub-questions (hypothesis, predictions) and links to literature are provided, ensuring that subsequent analysis continually refers back to the RQ.

**Limitations:**  The questionâs wording is rather convoluted and contains several clauses, making it less concise than ideal.  Furthermore, by relying solely on simulated data, the focus on âaccuracyâ is partially theoretical, which marginally weakens the alignment between question and achievable evidence.

**Overall judgment:**  Despite stylistic flaws, the RQ is unmistakably articulated and operationalised; connection to the investigation is strong.  This aligns with the 5-6 band; a score of **5/6** is appropriate.

**Strengths:**  Data tables, graphs and percentage-error calculations demonstrate direct linkage to the RQ.  The student analyses how scattering angle affects calculated radius, interprets intensity minima for diffraction, and compares both methodsâ precision.  Discussion of statistical vs systematic uncertainties shows sound evaluative effort.

**Limitations:**  Analysis is confined to simulated datasets of limited scope; no attempt is made to test sensitivity to input variations or to critique the validity of simulation algorithms.  Some conclusions (e.g., electron diffraction âmore frailâ) are asserted without quantitative backing beyond two data points.

**Overall judgment:**  The analysis is clear and relevant but lacks the depth and rigour characteristic of the top bands (10-12 or 7-9).  It best fits the upper end of the **4-6 band**; hence **6/12** is assigned.

**Strengths:**  The essay follows a logical chapter structure (Introduction, Methodology, Results, Analysis, Conclusion) with an accurate table of contents, page numbers, section headings and a complete bibliography.  Word count is stated and within the 4000-word limit.

**Limitations:**  Some formatting elements (e.g., over-long headings, occasional inconsistencies in equation numbering, partial duplication of figure captions) detract from overall polish.  Figure placement sometimes breaks text flow, and in-text citation style is not always consistent.

**Overall judgment:**  Structure is clear and generally conforms to EE conventions, but minor lapses preclude the top mark in the 3-4 band; **3/4** is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and accurately reflects the reportâs structure, assisting reader navigation.
The relation E=hc/Î» applies to photons, not electrons; clarify the proper de Broglie relation E=pc for matter waves and revise the application accordingly.
The Rayleigh criterion formula appears only in a figure; include its derivation in the text and discuss its relevance to resolving features at the nuclear scale.
The research question phrasing is overly long and complex, obscuring its focus. The student should rephrase succinctly to highlight the key variables and improve overall clarity.
Figure 1âs caption includes the source URL inline; standardize citation style by moving full URLs to the bibliography and using concise in-text references.
The title is excessively long and redundant. Consider condensing it to emphasize the core comparison between the scattering and diffraction methods for clarity and impact.
Word count is clearly stated and within the 4000-word limit, meeting formatting requirements.
Phrases like âmassive amount of experimental dataâ are imprecise; adopting quantitative descriptors would strengthen the academic rigor of background explanations.
The discussion references âFigure 10â when analyzing intensity minima but should refer to Figure 11; verify figure numbering to prevent reader confusion.
The statement of purpose is convoluted. Simplify the sentence to clearly articulate the investigationâs aim without excessive clauses.
Equation (1.1) numbering is inconsistently placed; apply a uniform style (e.g., right-justified numbering) to all displayed equations.

The impact of the strong nuclear force on the Coulomb-based radius derivation is mentioned qualitatively but lacks quantitative analysis; include an assessment of when strong interactions become non-negligible.
Equations 2.15 and 2.16 inconsistently use the factor 1.22; clarify its derivation from the Rayleigh criterion and ensure consistent application to both diameter and radius calculations.
Reference to Table 2 spans a page break; place the table on the same page as its first citation to maintain continuity and readability.
Figure 10âs caption uses italic formatting and deviates from the convention âFigure 10:â; adopt a consistent caption style and include the colon for clarity.

The claim âelectrostatic repulsion will become of attractionâ is conceptually incorrect; clarify that repulsive Coulomb force does not change sign and distinguish it from short-range strong nuclear attraction.
The relation âN â Ï â 1/(E_k)^2â is asserted without derivation; provide a step-by-step link between cross-section dependence on energy and the observed count rate.
Variable E_k is used without specifying units or conversion; explicitly state whether values are in MeV or joules and show conversions to maintain clarity.
The derived relation tan(Î¸/2)=r/(2b) is not applied to any dataset; include a worked example to demonstrate its use in determining impact parameters from scattering data.
The differential cross-section dÏ/dÎ© is introduced without defining the element dÎ© (solid angle); include this definition to clarify its geometric meaning.
Typographical error âRutherford-Geiger-Mardenâ should read âRutherford-Geiger-Marsdenâ; ensure accurate use of historical experiment names.
Justification for simulated datasets is brief; include calibration details, validation against known data, and discussion of potential simulation biases.
The scattering distribution dN(Î¸) is derived without discussing its valid angular range; comment on the small-angle approximation and its implications for data interpretation.
The final sentence of the analysis ends abruptly (âvalidity ofâ); ensure complete sentence structure to convey the intended argument fully.
Reference to Table 3 precedes the table placement, causing a page break; align the table with its discussion to preserve narrative flow.
In m=E/c^2, E must represent total energy (rest plus kinetic), not just kinetic energy; distinguish between rest mass energy and kinetic contributions for consistency.
Caption âDiffraction Pattern from the Electron Diffractionâ is tautological; revise to âElectron diffraction patternâ for conciseness and clarity.
Table 2 is well-structured with clear headers and units, effectively summarizing the parameters for nuclear radius calculations.
The reflections section describes outcomes but lacks analysis of how planning decisions influenced results; include evaluation of setbacks and adjustments to deepen reflective insight.
